speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Based on your recent labs and clinical evaluation, I’m diagnosing you with Chronic Kidney Disease Stage 3, confirmed as of April 21st.

speaker: patient
text: Thank you, Doctor. I’ve been feeling more fatigued lately, and my ankles have been a bit swollen.

speaker: doctor
text: I see — we’ll monitor that closely, especially given your kidney function. Let’s go over today’s vitals and medications.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate this morning is 51 beats per minute — slightly bradycardic, but stable for now.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve noticed my pulse feels slower sometimes, especially in the mornings.

speaker: patient
text: I also had some chest tightness yesterday evening — not sharp, but a dull pressure that lasted about five minutes while resting.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important — we’ll need to assess cardiac risk factors carefully, especially with CKD and your lipid profile.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose today is 9.6 mmol/L — elevated, though not acutely dangerous.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on insulin glargine 10 units nightly — let’s keep that unchanged for now.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is 99 mmHg — that’s elevated, and we’ll want to address it given your kidney disease.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m going to switch you from atorvastatin 20 mg to 40 mg once daily to better manage your cardiovascular risk.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — should I watch for any side effects with the higher dose?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — muscle aches or unusual fatigue; report those right away. Also, we’ll recheck liver enzymes in six weeks.

speaker: doctor
text: Interestingly, your blood glucose reading just before lunch was 11.5 mmol/L — higher than the fasting value we saw earlier.

speaker: patient
text: That makes sense — I had a sandwich with white bread and juice around 11 a.m., so it wasn’t fasting.

speaker: doctor
text: Exactly — postprandial spikes like that reinforce the need for consistent carb monitoring and possibly adjusting insulin timing later.

speaker: patient
text: My home BP cuff showed diastolic around 108 this morning — quite a jump from what you recorded earlier.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — your repeat office reading today shows diastolic at 108 mmHg, which confirms the upward trend we saw in your home log.

speaker: doctor
text: And your heart rate has dipped slightly again — now 50 bpm — still within acceptable range but worth tracking given your symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked my pulse just now, it was 51 bpm — same as that reading you took at the start.
